1: Chapter 7 opens the most extended narrative poem in Proverbs' prologue — a dramatic scene-study that renders concrete the abstract warnings of chapters 1–5. The father's command to store his words "within you" (ʾitti, with me/inside yourself) continues the Deuteronomic language of internalizing instruction (Deut 6:6). The imperative to "keep my words" echoes covenant-loyalty vocabulary; wisdom instruction is presented as a personal covenant between father and son.
2: The "apple of your eye" (ʾîšôn bat-ʿênekā, literally "the little man of the daughter of your eye") is the Hebrew idiom for the pupil — the most delicate and vital part of the eye, instinctively protected. Using it for wisdom's teaching gives the instruction a bodily urgency: guard it as you guard your sight itself. The command to "keep my teaching" (tôrātî) deploys the same word used for the Mosaic Torah, placing parental wisdom instruction within the larger framework of divine law-giving.
3: The instruction to "tie them on your fingers" and "inscribe them on the tablet of your heart" (lûaḥ libbeka) directly alludes to the covenant command of Deut 6:8–9 and 11:18–20 (and cf. Jer 31:33's new covenant promise). The tablet (lûaḥ) is the inscribed stone of Sinai; wisdom commands the same kind of writing, but on the body's interior. In ancient Israel, physical objects (phylacteries, doorpost inscriptions) made commands spatially present; Proverbs internalizes that presence into the heart.
4: Calling Wisdom "my sister" (ʾāḥôt) and understanding "my closest companion" (moddāʿ, an intimate kinsman) is family-bond language; the Hebrew moddāʿ denotes a close relative who bears relational obligations (cf. Ruth 2:1 of Boaz). In the ancient Near East, one married within the extended kin group; calling Wisdom "sister" signals the most intimate possible bond while naming a relationship that guards against the sexual stranger. The personification of Wisdom as a protective kin-figure counters the personification of Folly as a seductive stranger.
5: The function of the wisdom-sister is protective: she "will keep you from" (lišmārekā mê-) the strange woman and the seductive stranger (nokrîyyâ, foreigner). The Strange Woman (ʾiššâ zārâ) of chapters 1–9 is the antithesis of Woman Wisdom — both are personifications, both call out to the young man. The "flattering words" (ḥălāqâ, smooth, slippery speech) of the stranger recall the adulteress of 2:16 and 5:3 whose lips "drip honey" but whose path leads to Sheol. Ancient Near Eastern wisdom literature (Egyptian and Mesopotamian) similarly warns against the dangerous woman as the primary threat to the young man's formation.
6: The narrative frame shifts in v. 6 to autobiographical reporting: "Once at the window of my house I looked out through the lattice." The window-lattice (ʾešnab) is the screened aperture of Iron Age Israelite domestic architecture; it allows observation while maintaining privacy. The teacher becomes an eyewitness narrator — a technique that gives moral instruction the authority of lived experience rather than abstract precept. Comparable scenes appear in Egyptian wisdom literature (Instruction of Amenemope; Papyrus Insinger), where the teacher also claims observed experience.
7: The observer spots "among the naive" (pətāʾîm, the open-minded simple who have not yet committed to wisdom or folly) a young man described as ḥăsar-lēb (lacking heart/mind — the same phrase as the "senseless" of 24:30). He is unformed, making him the ideal dramatic subject for the lesson: his choice is still open. In ancient Israel's social world, young men's formation — before they established households of their own — was the explicit target audience of the entire wisdom tradition.
8: "Cutting through the street near her corner, heading down the road toward her house" — the spatial detail is morally diagnostic. The youth is not there by accident; šōʿeh (heading, marching, going purposefully) implies direction, not wandering. The "corner" (pinnāh) where the Strange Woman stations herself is the public intersection — a place of maximum foot traffic in ancient cities, where commerce, gossip, and solicitation all occurred. The movement toward "her house" (bêtāh) narrows the frame: the house is the destination of no return (v. 27).
9: The time-stamp is dense: "twilight" (bên hā-ʿarbayim, between the two evenings), "evening" (ʿereb), and "the black darkness of night" (ʾîšôn laylâ wă-ʾăpēlâ). Darkness in ancient Israelite culture was the time of moral disorder, of those who "hate the light" (Job 24:13–17); lawful commerce and community life happened by day at the gate. Moving toward her house in deepening darkness is moving out of the world of wisdom and covenant and into the domain of death. The three-fold darkness intensifies the danger with each phrase.
10: "A woman met him, dressed like a prostitute, her heart scheming" — the clothing (šît zônâ, dress/attire of a prostitute) is the visual signal of her public availability; but "her heart scheming" (nəṣurat lēb, literally "guarded/crafty of heart") reveals that her inner life is concealed, calculating. The prostitute's dress code in Iron Age Israel may be referenced here; some scholars note that veils and specific garments signaled status (cf. Gen 38:14–15 of Tamar). The external display masks an internal agenda, making her doubly deceptive.
11: "She is loud (hômiyyâ, tumultuous, noisy) and defiant (sōrāret, rebellious, stubborn)" — these are opposite the qualities of the ideal woman of chapter 31 and the self-controlled wife of chapter 12. sōrāret (from sārar, to be stubborn) is used of Israel's covenant rebellion (Jer 5:23; Hos 4:16); applied to the Strange Woman, it casts her behavior as a species of apostasy from right order. "Her feet never stay at home" — the house (bayit) is the locus of Israelite female domestic honor; her constant movement outside it violates social-moral expectations of the day.
12: The description of her as "now outside, now in the streets, lurking at every corner" creates a spatial omnipresence that mirrors the description of Woman Wisdom's public proclamation (8:1–3; 9:1–3). Both figures inhabit public space; the difference is their posture — Wisdom calls openly from the high ground and the gates, while the Strange Woman lurks (ʾārab, to lie in wait, to ambush) at corners. The same verb ʾārab is used of military ambush (Josh 8:2, 4); the seductive woman is a predator, not simply a temptress.
13: "She grabbed him and kissed him" — the physical aggression is notable; typically in ANE literature it is the man who initiates physical contact. Here she is the pursuer. "Said to him with a brazen face" (hēʿēzâ pānêhā, she strengthened/hardened her face): a hardened face is the physical sign of shamelessness — the opposite of the blushing, modest face expected of Israelite women. Jeremiah uses the same phrase of Israel's shameless apostasy (Jer 3:3). Her boldness is covenant-defying. The kiss precedes the speech, establishing physical intimacy before the verbal seduction begins.
14: "I had fellowship offerings (šəlāmîm) to make; today I paid my vows" — this is a culturally specific detail that modern readers easily miss. The šəlāmîm (peace/fellowship offerings) were sacrificial meals at which the worshiper was required to eat the meat the same day or the next day (Lev 7:15–18). Having just offered sacrifices, she has a house full of fresh meat that must be consumed by nightfall — hence the urgency of her invitation. The sacrifice frames the seduction in religious terms: she has just been at the sanctuary, the religious legitimacy of the occasion covering the moral disorder of what follows.
15: "That is why I came out to meet you — I was looking for you and here you are" — the grammar of wāʾimṣāʾekā (I found you) uses the language of wisdom-seeking: in 8:17 Wisdom says "those who seek me will find me"; here the Strange Woman uses the same vocabulary of finding to describe her predatory searching. The language of earnest seeking is co-opted by seduction. Her claim that she specifically sought him personalizes the encounter and flatters the young man's sense of being specially chosen — a classic manipulation technique.
16: "Colored blankets and fine linen from Egypt" — the bed is prepared with imported luxury textiles. Egyptian linen was proverbially fine and expensive (cf. Prov 31:24); the display of Egyptian goods signals wealth, cosmopolitan sophistication, and access to long-distance trade networks. In the ancient Near East, luxurious bedding was a sign of high social status; the scene is designed to make the encounter seem an elevation in status for the young man. The exotic materials also carry a hint of the foreign — nokrîyyâ (foreign woman) is her designation — making Egypt part of the danger's texture.
17: Myrrh, aloes, and cinnamon — all imported aromatics used in perfumery, medicine, and sacred contexts. Myrrh (mōr) was used in the anointing oil of Exodus 30:23 and is the scent of the beloved in Song of Songs 1:13; 3:6; 4:14. Aloes (ʾahālôt) are aromatic wood from southeast Asia; cinnamon (qinnāmôn) is the bark of a Sri Lankan tree that entered ancient Mediterranean trade via Arabia and Phoenicia. The scent-palette is deliberately evocative of the erotic poetry of the Song of Songs — the Strange Woman uses the vocabulary of rightful love to frame illicit encounter.
18: "Come, let us drink our fill of love" (nirweh dōdîm, let us be saturated with loves/caresses) — dôdîm (plural of dôd, beloved, caresses) is the intimate vocabulary of the Song of Songs (1:2, 4; 4:10; 5:1; 7:12). The Strange Woman deliberately mirrors the language of the Song to make illicit love seem identical to rightful love — or perhaps more thrilling. "Until morning" implies an all-night encounter, the absence of her husband providing the window. The appeal is to pleasure, not commitment.
19: "My husband is not home" — the Hebrew hāʾîš (the man) is used rather than baʿlî (my lord/husband), a slightly distancing term. He "has gone on a long trip" (derek merāḥôq, a road far away) — the realities of Iron Age commerce required extended travel: merchants, officials, and craftsmen traveled trade routes that took weeks or months. The husband's absence on business is the social precondition for her availability. The detail authenticates the scenario culturally while providing the legal cover she deploys to reduce the young man's fear of being caught.
20: "He took his money with him" (ṣərôr hakkesef lāqaḥ bəyādô, he took the bag of money in his hand) — he is a merchant, a man of commerce, carrying capital. "He will not be back until the full moon" (yôm hakkēse) — a specific calendrical reference: the full moon (15th of the month in the lunar calendar) establishes a known window. She knows exactly how long she has. The specificity of the time-frame is the final detail that makes her case seem reasonable and risk-free — a calculated manipulation that uses the young man's economic rationality against him.
21: "With her many persuasive words she led him astray" — liqḥāh (she took him, she led him) is the same verb used of wisdom's instruction being "taken" or received (1:3; 8:10). The Strange Woman has her own form of pedagogy: an alternative wisdom education that re-routes the student toward death. Her words are described as laqqāh (convincing, persuasive — what wisdom also offers, 1:5; 4:2). The structural parallel is deliberate: seduction is a parody of wisdom-formation, using the same means toward opposite ends.
22: "He followed her at once — like an ox heading to slaughter, like a fool walking into punishment" — the animal similes domesticate the tragedy: the ox does not know it is heading to death; the young man is equally unknowing. The fool (ʾěwîl) walking into musar (discipline/punishment) — the very term for wisdom's corrective instruction — inverts it: what should lead to wisdom here leads to destruction. The similes belong to wisdom's characteristic mode of teaching by analogy, used here with bitter irony: the young man who should be learning from the animals is being compared unfavorably to them.
23: "Until an arrow pierces his liver" — the liver (kābed) was considered the seat of the emotions and inner life in ancient Mesopotamian physiology; to pierce the liver was the mortal wound. Divination was performed on animal livers (haruspicy), suggesting the liver's cosmological importance. The bird-and-snare image follows: the bird "does not know that his life is the price" (bənap̄šô lō yēdāʿ, he does not know it is for his life/soul) — nephesh here as the life-stake. The chapter that began with "keep my commands and live" (v. 2) ends with death as the cost of ignoring them.
24: The shift back to direct address (ʾattā bānîm, now, children) marks the end of the narrative and the opening of the application. The plural "children" broadens the address from the individual naive young man to the whole class of the teachable; the seduction-narrative has become a case-study for the group. "Pay close attention to what I am saying" (qāšîbû ʾimrê-pî, give ear to the words of my mouth) — the imperatives echo the opening of ch. 5 (5:1) and 8:6; the teacher's address-formula is itself a wisdom convention reaching back to Egyptian instruction texts.
25: "Do not let your heart wander toward her ways" — the heart (lēb) is the navigational organ in Proverbs; wisdom shapes it so that it chooses the right path instinctively. The command uses yēšeṭ (let it turn aside, wander; from šāṭâ) for the dangerous drift of the unguarded heart. "Do not go astray on her paths" — tiʿû bənətîbôtêhā (stray in her paths): the paths (nəṭîbôt) of the Strange Woman lead to death, just as wisdom's paths lead to life (cf. 3:17). The spatial metaphor that organizes the entire prologue — two paths, two destinations — reaches its climax here.
26: "She has brought down many victims; a long line of powerful men have been destroyed by her" — rabbîm (many) and ʿăṣumîm (strong, powerful) specify that it is not only the weak or foolish who fall: even the strong are not immune. This functions as the chapter's most sobering note: the naive young man of the narrative was merely one instance. Historical wisdom traditions recall heroes who fell to sexual temptation (Samson; Amnon) as warnings; the "powerful men" the Strange Woman has destroyed may gesture toward these known figures from Israel's story.
27: "Her house is the road to Sheol; it leads to the inner chambers of death" — the spatial journey that began in v. 6–9 (street → corner → her street → approaching her house) now reaches its terminus: her house is not a house at all but a road into the underworld. Šəʾôl (the realm of the dead) and ḥadrê māwet (inner rooms/chambers of death) form a chiasm with the opening of ch. 7: wisdom's commands lead to life (v. 2); the Strange Woman's house leads to death. The architectural image — house as passage into death — recurs in ancient Near Eastern myth (Inanna's descent through the gates of the underworld).
